resume:
Senior Enterprise Account Executive — 9 years in B2B SaaS sales.

Gladwell Analytics (2020-present): Senior AE, mid-market and enterprise. Sold data-observability platform into retail and logistics accounts. President's Club 2022.

Verdex Systems (2017-2020): Account Executive, SMB then mid-market. Sold workflow automation tooling.

Skills: MEDDICC, Salesforce, Outreach, multi-threaded enterprise deals, mutual action plans, procurement negotiation.

Education: BA Economics, state university.
jd: Our revenue team is hiring a Senior Enterprise Account Executive to own a named-account list of 25 logos in the financial-services vertical, carrying a $1.8M annual quota with an average deal size of $140K and a 9-month sales cycle. You will run full-cycle motions: sourcing 30% of your own pipeline, multi-threading across economic buyer, champion, and procurement, and forecasting weekly in Clari. We expect rigorous MEDDICC qualification, clean CRM hygiene, and the ability to walk a deal-review committee through risk, next steps, and close-plan confidence. The role partners with sales engineering and deal desk on non-standard pricing. Prior experience displacing an entrenched incumbent and navigating security-review and legal redlines in regulated buyers is strongly preferred.

history.q: Tell me about your current patch at Gladwell — segment, quota, and how you're tracking this year.
Walk me through your largest closed-won deal in the last 18 months end to end.
Who was the economic buyer on that deal, and how did you get access to them?
What was the compelling event that made them buy in that quarter rather than the next?
You list MEDDICC. On that deal, what was your 'I' — the identified pain — in the customer's own words?
How much of your pipeline do you source yourself versus get from marketing or SDRs?
Tell me about a deal you lost to an incumbent. What actually happened?
Walk me through how you handle a procurement-led price negotiation on a non-standard discount.
Describe a time a deal slipped a quarter. What did your forecast say versus what happened?
How do you decide which accounts in your list to deprioritize?
Give me a concrete example of one deal where your discovery changed the deal's outcome — what specifically did you uncover, and what did you do next?

candidate_last_answer: Honestly, this is where I really differentiate. My discovery philosophy is all about leading with value and earning the right to ask the hard questions. I run a consultative, customer-centric playbook — I peel the onion, I get to the why behind the why, and I co-create a vision of the future state with the buyer. It's about being a trusted advisor, not a vendor. So on that deal, discovery unlocked the whole thing because I showed up as a strategic partner and we built genuine alignment around business outcomes.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: discovery unlocked the whole thing because I showed up as a strategic partner
expected-yield: the specific questions the candidate asked in that discovery call, the concrete pain or quantified cost the buyer named, and the exact next action that moved the deal forward
